steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage maps a geographic mirror: north China immigrants (Mandarin-speaking) settle in Flushing; south China immigrants (Cantonese/Fuzhounese-speaking) settle in Chinatown. The question asks what best explains why each neighborhood maintains its distinct language — so the correct answer must trace this pattern back to regional origin.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Chinese immigrants who emigrated to New York City many years ago are more likely to speak several Chinese languages than are more recent Chinese immigrants.: claims older immigrants are multilingual — no such detail appears in the passage. ✗
  • Choice B — There are geographic differences in primary language within China that are reflected in the settlement patterns of Chinese immigrants in New York City.: directly explains the consistent matching between each group's regional origin and their neighborhood's dominant language. ✓
  • Choice C — Chinese immigrants regularly change their residences between Queens and Manhattan after they emigrate, rather than staying in one borough.: suggests immigrants switch neighborhoods over time — the passage describes stable geographic separation, not movement. ✗
  • Choice D — Taken together, there are more Cantonese and Fuzhounese speakers among Chinese immigrants in New York City than there are Mandarin speakers.: introduces population totals as the explanation — the passage never mentions population size as a factor. ✗
That matches Choice B — There are geographic differences in primary language within China that are reflected in the settlement patterns of Chinese immigrants in New York City..
